name: NATO phonetic alphabet
description: The NATO/ICAO radiotelephony spelling alphabet — the code words used to spell letters unambiguously over radio. Tested both ways (letter→code word and code word→letter), tracked separately.
primer:
The **NATO/ICAO radiotelephony spelling alphabet** assigns one distinctive **code word** to each letter A–Z so they can't be confused over a noisy radio (e.g. 'B' = *Bravo*). The words were chosen to be recognisable to speakers of many languages.

**Full mapping — letter → code word:**

- **A** — Alfa (spelled 'Alfa', not Alpha, so non-English speakers keep the 'f')
- **B** — Bravo
- **C** — Charlie
- **D** — Delta
- **E** — Echo
- **F** — Foxtrot
- **G** — Golf
- **H** — Hotel
- **I** — India
- **J** — Juliett (double-t so French speakers sound the final 't')
- **K** — Kilo
- **L** — Lima
- **M** — Mike
- **N** — November
- **O** — Oscar
- **P** — Papa
- **Q** — Quebec
- **R** — Romeo
- **S** — Sierra
- **T** — Tango
- **U** — Uniform
- **V** — Victor
- **W** — Whiskey
- **X** — X-ray
- **Y** — Yankee
- **Z** — Zulu (also the radio name for UTC / 'Zulu time')

**Special cases:** *Alfa* and *Juliett* use non-standard spellings on purpose; *Zulu* doubles as the name for UTC time.

cards.note: Spelled 'Alfa' (not Alpha) so non-English speakers don't drop the 'ph'.
Spelled with a double-t so French speakers sound the final 't'.
Also the radio name for UTC / 'Zulu time'.
